title: Spaghetti al pomodoro
subtitle: Hazan's three-ingredient tomato sauce, butter, onion, tinned tomatoes
excerpt: Spaghetti al pomodoro here means Marcella Hazan's version: tinned tomatoes, a halved onion, and butter, simmered for 45 minutes without a stir then discarded of the onion. The sauce is sweet, round, and deeply tomatoey. No garlic, no olive oil, no herbs. Three ingredients, and nothing to fix.
sourceNotes:
• Marcella Hazan, *Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking* (1992). This recipe is Hazan's tomato sauce with butter and onion, one of the most reprinted recipes in modern food writing. The method is followed faithfully here: tinned tomatoes, a halved onion, and butter cooked together for 45 minutes.
• Pellegrino Artusi, *La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene* (1891, public domain). Background on the Italian tomato sauce tradition as it developed from the late nineteenth century.

content.text: sauce is three ingredients: tinned tomatoes, a halved onion, and butter. No garlic, no olive oil, no bay leaf, no herb. You put them in a pan, cook for 45 minutes without stirring much, fish out the onion, and you have a sauce that is sweeter and rounder than almost anything you will make with more effort. The onion goes in to flavour the sauce and comes out before serving. It is the oldest trick in the book.

Use good tinned tomatoes. This recipe has nowhere to hide a flat, acidic tin. San Marzano are reliable; if they are not available, any whole plum tomatoes from a trusted brand are fine. The butter provides the round, creamy quality that keeps the sauce from being sharp. Don't substitute olive oil.

items.name: Table salt
items.prepNote: to taste, plus for pasta water

content.text: The only equipment is a medium saucepan and patience. The sauce does not need supervision once it is at a gentle simmer.

Put {{tinned-tomatoes}} of whole tinned tomatoes in a medium saucepan. Crush them with a spoon or your hand. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, cut into a few pieces. Add {{onion}} halved onion, cut-side down. Season lightly with salt. Set over a medium heat.

Bring to a gentle simmer, then cook, uncovered, for 45 minutes. Stir occasionally, every 10 minutes is enough. The sauce should reduce by about a third and darken slightly in colour. Taste and adjust the salt.
Remove and discard the onion. The sauce can be left chunky or passed through a coarse sieve for a smoother result.

Bring a large saucepan of salted water to a rolling boil. Cook {{pasta-dried}} of pasta according to the packet. Reserve a mug of pasta water before draining. Toss the pasta with the sauce in the pan, adding pasta water to loosen as needed. Serve with grated parmesan if using.

items.fix: Cook for the full 45 minutes. The long simmer sweetens the tomatoes and rounds the acid. A pinch of salt at the start also helps. If the tomatoes were particularly acidic, a very small pinch of sugar will correct it without making the sauce sweet.
items.cause: The tomatoes were not reduced long enough, or the tin was low-quality.

items.fix: Keep the heat at a gentle simmer, small bubbles breaking the surface, not a rolling boil. If it catches, add a splash of water and lower the heat. The sauce should not brown.
items.symptom: Sauce has reduced too much and is catching on the pan

items.fix: The 55 g is Hazan's specified amount for two tins. This is more butter than most people expect, and that is the point. Don't reduce the quantity.
items.cause: Too little butter, or it was added too early and separated.
items.symptom: Sauce does not taste of butter

items.fix: Continue cooking, uncovered, until the sauce reduces to the right consistency. It should coat a spoon without running straight off.
items.cause: The simmer was too gentle, or the tin had a lot of liquid.
items.symptom: Sauce is too thin after 45 minutes

content.text: Marcella Hazan published this recipe in *Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking* in 1992. It is often called the recipe that changed how English-speaking home cooks think about Italian tomato sauce. The mix of butter (not olive oil) and a whole onion (lifted out before serving) sounded too simple to work. It worked. The recipe has been reprinted and shared so widely that it is now almost common property. Cook it once and it tends to stay in the rotation.
